text: Spatial multi-omics: deciphering technological landscape of integration of multi-omics and its applications. Xiaojie Liu, Ting Peng, Miaochun Xu, Shitong Lin, Bai Hu, Tian Chu, Binghan Liu, Yashi Xu, Wencheng Ding, Li Li, Canhui Cao, Peng Wu. Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2024) 17:72. Abstract: The emergence of spatial multi-omics has helped address the limitations of single-cell sequencing, which often leads to the loss of spatial context among cell populations. Integrated analysis of the genome, transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, and epigenome has enhanced our understanding of cell biology and molecular diseases. Moreover, this approach offers profound insights into the interactions between intracellular and intercellular molecular mechanisms involved in the development, physiology, and pathogenesis of human diseases. In this comprehensive review, we examine current advancements in multi-omics technologies, focusing on their evolution and refinement over the past decade, including improvements in throughput and resolution, modality integration, and accuracy. We also discuss the pivotal contributions of spatial multi-omics in revealing spatial heterogeneity, constructing detailed spatial atlases, deciphering spatial crosstalk in tumor immunology, and advancing translational research and cancer therapy through precise spatial mapping. Keywords: Spatial multi-omics, Heterogeneity, Spatial-specific atlas, Lineage tracking, Crosstalk, New therapy, Reproduction. Introduction: Single-cell sequencing has been instrumental in providing detailed insights into gene expression at the individual cell level for decades. However, despite its advantages, it often fails to retain critical spatial information. To overcome this, spatial multi-omics has emerged as a transformative technology, enabling the precise localization of cells within tissues and quantitative measurement of gene expression in situ. This advancement marks an important technological breakthrough in life sciences and biomedicine, with wide-ranging applications. In this review, we trace the developmental timeline of spatial multi-omics technologies, highlighting their evolution and substantial contributions to modern science.

section: Technologies for Spatial Omics Part 1: Spatial Transcriptomics (Image-based)
text: Technologies for spatial omics: Spatial mono-omics, such as spatial transcriptomics, builds the foundation. In this section, we introduce key spatial mono-omics techniques and their role in advancing spatial multi-omics. Spatial Transcriptomics: It has significantly enhanced our understanding of cellular organization and intra-tissue interactions. Recent advancements focus on increasing detectable genes/proteins, enhancing sensitivity and resolution, and expanding the analyzed area. 1) Image-based in situ transcriptomics. This primarily includes fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) and in situ sequencing (ISS). Recent advancements feature highly multiplexed single-molecule FISH (smFISH), which uses reverse complementary oligo probes conjugated with fluorophores for precise mRNA quantification and localization. To address issues like optical crowding, methods like multiplexed error-robust FISH (MERFISH) and sequential FISH (seqFISH) have been developed. Both ISH and ISS provide similar transcriptomic information, with ISS-based methods directly reading nucleotide sequences within tissues. In ISS, padlock probes containing gene-specific barcodes are ligated and amplified via rolling circle amplification (RCA). Methods like Fluorescent In Situ Sequencing (FISSEQ) and Spatially resolved transcript amplicon readout mapping (STARmap) employ different chemistries for in situ detection. BaristaSeq is an optimized padlock probe-based technique. Both ISS and ISH methods require image processing to generate gene expression matrices. Computational frameworks like JSTA and SSAM help with cell segmentation and type annotation.

section: Technologies for Spatial Omics Part 2: Spatial Transcriptomics (NGS & LCM) and Spatial Genomics
text: 2) Oligonucleotide-based spatial barcoding followed by next-generation sequencing (NGS). NGS represents a significant improvement, offering cost-effective, rapid sequencing with higher throughput. Prominent NGS platforms include 454 (pyrosequencing), Illumina/Solexa, and SOLiD. Third-generation sequencing technologies like PacBio and Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) offer longer read lengths and reduce PCR bias. 3) Laser capture microdissection (LCM). LCM is a microscope-guided cutting system that uses UV light to accurately isolate specific tissues or cells of interest from complex structures, overcoming challenges posed by samples unsuitable for conventional RNA-seq. Spatial Genomics: The proper functioning of tissues relies on precise spatial organization. In cancer, tumor cells exhibit intratumor genetic heterogeneity. In situ genome sequencing (IGS) and slide-DNA-seq are two key methods. IGS expands non-targeted genomic samples in a natural spatial environment, creating an in situ sequencing library. Slide-DNA-seq enables spatially resolved sequencing of DNA from intact tissues by generating a spatial index array with DNA barcodes corresponding to specific locations. It is useful for large-scale mapping of tumor evolution and clonal heterogeneity. Current methods for characterizing chromatin states or DNA within tissues on a large spatial scale are still emerging.

section: Technologies for Spatial Omics Part 3: Spatial Proteomics, Epigenomics, and Metabolomics
text: Spatial Proteomics: Proteomics involves the large-scale study of proteins. Targeted localization and mislocalization of proteins are crucial in cell biology and disease. Two primary approaches are used: 1) Mass spectrometry (MS)-based methods: These involve the enrichment and quantification of proteins through biochemical fractionation. MS analysis combined with multivariate statistics and machine learning is used to infer protein locations. Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) combines AI-driven image analysis with ultra-high-sensitivity MS. 2) Imaging-based methods: These allow visualization of protein distribution at single-cell resolution but are limited by antibody availability. MS and imaging methods complement each other. Spatial Epigenomics: This examines modifications to DNA sequence and chromatin structure that regulate gene activity, such as histone modifications and DNA methylation. Epigenomic MERFISH combined with Cleavage Under Targets and Tagmentation (CUT&Tag) enables mapping of epigenomic loci in tissues. Spatial Metabolomics: Metabolites play crucial roles in cellular activities. 1) Targeted metabolomics analyzes specific subsets of compounds using methods like FT-MS and NMR. 2) Untargeted metabolomics aims for global analysis, with LC/MS being the most common platform. Data analysis addresses complexity through grouping metabolites and annotating ion species. Metabolomics provides unique insights into phenotypic characteristics.

section: Integration of Spatial Multi-Omics Part 1: Transcriptomics with (Epi)Genomics and Proteomics
text: Integration of spatial multi-omics enables simultaneous analysis of multiple data modalities (transcriptomics, proteomics, genomics, epigenomics, metabolomics) from the same tissue section. Integration of spatial transcriptomics and (epi)genomics: Spatial ATAC&RNA-seq and spatial CUT&Tag-RNA-seq revolutionize genome-wide co-mapping of the epigenome and transcriptome. These integrate microfluidic deterministic barcoding in tissue (DBiT) strategies. Spatial-ATAC-seq enables high-resolution mapping of chromatin accessibility. Spatial-CUT&Tag analyzes spatial histone modification profiling. DBiT-seq creates a 2D grid of spatially barcoded tissue pixels. These techniques have been applied to co-map mouse and human brains. DNA-seqFISH+ can be applied for studying spatial genome structure. Integration of spatial proteomics and transcriptomics: Few platforms successfully integrate these. Spatial Multi-Omics (SM-Omics) is an automated framework for high-throughput transcriptome and antibody-based spatial tissue profiling. NanoString GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler (DSP) facilitates high-plex profiling at protein and RNA levels. Spatial co-indexing of transcriptomes and epitopes (Spatial-CITE-seq) offers high-plex protein and whole-transcriptome co-mapping.

section: Integration of Spatial Multi-Omics Part 2: Transcriptomics with Metabolomics and Multi-Modal Integration
text: Integration of spatial transcriptomics and metabolomics: A spatial multimodal analysis (SMA) protocol combines spatially resolved transcriptomics and mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) in a single tissue slice. This approach revealed associations in brain injury and oral squamous cell carcinoma studies. Integration of spatial transcriptomics, genomics, and proteomics: Multi-omics in situ pairwise sequencing (MiP-seq) is a high-throughput targeted in situ sequencing technique that simultaneously detects multiplexed DNA, RNA, proteins, and biomolecules at subcellular resolution. It uses a pairwise-sequencing strategy and dual barcoded padlock probes, increasing decoding capacity and reducing sequencing rounds. MiP-seq has been applied to mouse brain tissue and virus-infected cells, demonstrating versatility in multi-omics in situ analysis.

section: Applications of Spatial Multi-Omics Part 1: Atlas, Heterogeneity, and Crosstalk
text: Applications of spatial multi-omics: 1) Deciphering spatial-specific atlas production of molecular and cellular profiles. A comprehensive atlas is essential for understanding biology and disease. Examples include atlases of the human temporal gyrus, human maternal-fetal interface, cardiac remodeling, and mouse embryos/brain using spatial ATAC-RNA-seq and CUT&Tag-RNA-seq. 2) Spatial multi-omics decodes spatial-based heterogeneity in human diseases. Tumor heterogeneity is crucial for adaptation and treatment resistance. Studies in metastatic prostate cancer (using spatial transcriptomics/proteomics), non-small cell lung cancer (using MALDI-TOF, CyCIF, sequencing), glioblastoma (using MALDI-MSI and proteomics), and gastric cancer (using spatial metabolomics/transcriptomics) have quantified intratumor heterogeneity and linked it to outcomes. 3) Understanding spatial-related crosstalk in tumor immunology. Spatial multi-omics provides new perspectives on complex interactions within the tumor microenvironment (TME). Studies in gastric cancer identified a distinct tumor-neighboring tissue interface with specific metabolic and inflammatory features. Research in glioblastoma and intestinal damage used spatial multi-omics to quantify cell-cell interactions within defined spatial regions.

section: Applications of Spatial Multi-Omics Part 2: Lineage Tracking, New Therapies, and Reproduction
text: 4) Spatial trajectory and lineage tracking in human diseases. Lineage tracking is crucial for studying developmental trajectories. Spatial transcriptomics enables comprehensive analysis and reveals spatiotemporal relationships. Applications include studying human embryonic limb development, macrophage differentiation, and renal fibrosis pathogenesis. 5) Investigation of new therapies via spatial multi-omics. Integrating spatial multi-omics could enhance understanding of tumor metabolic regulation, offering new therapeutic targets. Examples include: identifying microbial metabolite TMAO as a potential therapy for triple-negative breast cancer; mapping metabolic changes in breast cancer metastasis; using spatial metabolomics/transcriptomics to study compounds improving nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD); defining ferroptosis landscapes in multiple sclerosis (MS) for therapeutic targeting; and using spatial transcriptomics/proteomics to understand the effects of vedolizumab in ulcerative colitis. 6) Multi-omics in reproduction and development research. Spatial techniques can examine interactions within the natural tissue environment of the reproductive system. Studies have profiled remodeling of the female reproductive tract, generated a multi-omics atlas of the developing human fetal cerebellum, and provided an atlas of the developing human spinal cord.

Perspectives: The field aims for higher resolution, deeper coverage, greater multiplexity, and enhanced versatility. Key steps include data normalization, quality control, and integration. Developing benchmarks for integration algorithms and open datasets is crucial. A thorough understanding of specific microenvironments is key for advancing personalized precision medicine. Conclusions: The integration of multi-omics with spatial analysis is a rapidly evolving field with great promise. It enables a deeper understanding of complex biological systems. However, integrating data presents technical and interpretative challenges, necessitating advanced computational tools. References: The review cites numerous key studies, including Baysoy et al. on single-cell multi-omics (Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol. 2023), Rao et al. on exploring tissue architecture (Nature. 2021), Marx V. on Method of the Year (Nat Methods. 2021), Zhao et al. on spatial genomics (Nature. 2022), Vicari et al. on spatial multimodal analysis (Nat Biotechnol. 2023), and Kuppe et al. on spatial multi-omic map of myocardial infarction (Nature. 2022), among many others covering technologies and applications across various diseases.
